TDX Ticketing - Getting Started: Tickets in TeamDynamix (TDX) have key concepts including classifications, types, status values, priority, and assignment. The main ticket classifications are Incident, Major Incident, Problem, Change, Release, and Service Request.

TDX Creating Tickets: Technicians can create tickets multiple ways in Work Management. For walk-ins or phone calls, go to TDX > New Ticket and fill in the requester, classification, service, title, and description. Different incident types have different creation methods.

TDX Ticket Assignment and Ownership: Tickets can be assigned to a specific technician or group. Technicians can Take Ownership or Add to My Work list. Key concepts: primary responsibility, responsibility, and current responsibility are different fields.

TDX Updating Tickets: Technicians can update a ticket by changing status, adding comments and time entries, managing contacts, adjusting SLAs, reclassifying, and editing details. Always add a comment when changing status to document what was done.

TDX Ticket Classifications - Differences: Incident = unplanned interruption or degradation of a service. Major Incident = high impact incident affecting many users. Problem = root cause of one or more incidents. Change = planned modification to infrastructure or services. Release = deployment of approved changes. Service Request = request for something new (access, equipment, information).

TDX Parent/Child Tickets: Complex issues can be broken into a Parent ticket with multiple Child tickets. The parent tracks the overall issue while children track individual tasks or subtasks. Resolving all children does not auto-resolve the parent.
TDX Ticket Tasks: Tasks can be added to tickets to break resolution into steps. Each task can be assigned to different technicians. Tasks have their own status tracking.

TDX Merging Tickets: Duplicate tickets about the same issue can be merged individually or in bulk. Merging combines the tickets into one and closes the duplicates with a reference to the merged ticket.
TDX Scheduled Tickets: Recurring maintenance tasks can be set up as Scheduled Tickets that automatically create on a defined schedule. Useful for preventative maintenance and regular checks.

TDX SLA (Service Level Agreements): SLAs define response and resolution time targets. Elements include response time, resolution time, business hours schedule, and escalation rules. Tickets approaching SLA deadlines should be prioritized.

TDX Viewing and Organizing Work: Use the My Work app to see tickets assigned to you. Use saved Ticket Searches to filter tickets by status, group, type, etc. Dashboards provide at-a-glance views of team workload.

TDX Requestor Fields: The Requestor field on a ticket identifies who submitted the request. Typeahead search lets you find requesters by name, StarID, or email. Requestor data auto-fills from the directory.
TDX Alerts and Follow-up: Set Alerts on tickets to get notified of changes. Follow-up surveys can be sent to requesters after ticket resolution to gather feedback on the service experience.
TDX Archived Tickets: Resolved and closed tickets are eventually archived. Archived tickets are read-only but can be searched and viewed for historical reference.

TDX Converting Tickets: Tickets can be converted to Project Requests if the work is too large for a standard ticket. Project Requests can then be converted to full Projects with tasks, milestones, and resource planning.

TDX Asset Fields: Tickets can have assets linked to them. Asset fields support lookup and search to find equipment by name, tag, or serial number. Linking assets helps track which devices are related to reported issues.

TDX Multiple Editors Warning: If multiple technicians open the same ticket simultaneously, TDX will warn of concurrent edits to prevent data conflicts. Last save wins, so coordinate with your team to avoid overwriting each others updates.

TDX Client Portal: Requesters can submit tickets, track progress, communicate via comments, manage alerts, and withdraw requests through the TDX Client Portal at services.smsu.edu/TDClient/180/Portal/
TDX Keyboard Shortcuts: A keyboard shortcut exists to close the ticket side panel for faster navigation. Check the TDX documentation for the current shortcut key.

TDX Contact Field: Tickets can have Contacts added beyond the primary requestor. Contacts can be configured to view the ticket from the Client Portal, useful for supervisors or affected parties who need visibility.

TDX Getting Started with Tickets: Tickets in TeamDynamix are used by technicians to restore service to users who have experienced issues (incident management), fulfill service requests, and handle problem, change, and release management processes. Tickets require Technician license or higher and access to Work Management.

TDX Where to Access Tickets: End Users go to Client Portal > Services. Technicians go to Work Management > Ticketing Application to view assigned tickets, update status, add time and comments. The My Work app shows all assigned work across applications.

TDX Ticket Lifecycle - Creating: Tickets can be created 5 ways: 1) Customer submits via Service Catalog, 2) Customer emails the service area via Email Monitor, 3) Technician creates manually in Work Management on behalf of customer, 4) Bulk import, 5) API call.

TDX Ticket Automation Rules: As soon as a ticket is created, Automation Rules run automatically. They can assign the ticket to a person or group, apply a workflow, assign an SLA, set attribute values, and send notifications. Exception: imported tickets do not trigger Automation Rules.

TDX Ticket Updating: Common reasons to update a ticket: 1) Change the Status (New, In Progress, On Hold, Resolved, Closed), 2) Notify the Requestor via comments visible in Client Portal, 3) Add Internal Notes (only visible to technicians), 4) Record Time spent working. Always document what you did before changing status.

TDX Communicating with Requestors: External comments are visible to the requestor in the Client Portal. Internal notes are only visible to technicians. Use internal notes to document troubleshooting steps without exposing internal details to requestors.

TDX Editing Tickets: Edit a ticket when the client used the wrong form, service needs to be changed, requestor needs to be updated, or custom attribute values need modification. Editing permissions may be limited based on whether the technician is the primary responsible party.

TDX Closing Tickets: When work is complete, update to Resolved or Closed. Resolved = solution provided, awaiting requestor confirmation. Closed = ticket completely finished, no further action required.

TDX Ticket Tasks: Add tasks to a ticket to break complex work into steps or engage multiple teams. Ticket tasks are subtasks. When to use: breaking down complex work, engaging multiple teams, tracking sequential steps. Best practice: assign ticket to the owning group and use tasks to coordinate other teams.

TDX Ticket Workflows: Workflows guide tickets through structured multi-step processes. Unlike tasks (work to be done), workflow steps represent stages in a business process. Common uses: change management approvals, multi-level authorization, conditional routing, automated notifications.

TDX Parent-Child Tickets: Parent-child relationships connect related tickets across different classifications. Use when: linking multiple incidents to a single problem ticket, connecting related changes to a release, managing major incidents with multiple components.

TDX Scheduled Tickets: Scheduled tickets automate creation of recurring work on a defined schedule. Benefits: automatically create tickets for routine maintenance, ensure consistent tracking of cyclical tasks, reduce manual effort.

TDX Service Level Agreements SLA: SLAs establish response and resolution timeframes based on ticket priority. Higher priority tickets get shorter timeframes. SLA violations can trigger notifications to management. Impact + Urgency = Priority which determines SLA timeframes.

TDX ITIL Ticket Classifications - Full Definitions: Incident = unplanned interruption to IT service or reduction in quality. Major Incident = significantly affects business, demands response beyond routine process, affects critical systems or reputation. Problem = cause of one or more incidents, root cause investigation. Change = addition/modification/removal of anything affecting IT services. Release = collection of hardware/software/docs required to implement approved changes. Service Request = request for information, advice, standard change, or access - handled by service desk, no RFC needed.

TDX ITIL Classification Use Case Example: 50 WiFi incidents get rolled into one Problem ticket about infrastructure. A Change ticket is opened to upgrade WiFi. A Release ticket is created to deploy the new infrastructure and documentation.

TDX Classification Hierarchy for Parent-Child: In TDX, a Release can be parent to Changes, Problems, Major Incidents, and Incidents. A Change can be parent to Problems and Incidents. This hierarchy determines valid parent-child relationships.

TDX Ticket Types vs Classifications: Classification defines the service management process (Incident, Problem, Change, etc). Ticket Type provides more granular categorization within that process. Examples of ticket types: Hardware Issue, Software Installation, Account Access, Password Reset, Network Connectivity.

TDX Status Values Explained: New = just created, not yet reviewed. In Progress = work actively being performed. On Hold = work paused, waiting for info or dependencies. Resolved = issue addressed, solution provided. Closed = completed, no further action. Organizations can customize status values.

TDX Impact Urgency Priority: Impact = effect on business (financial loss, number of affected users, severity of disruption). Urgency = how quickly it needs resolution (time sensitivity, business deadlines, rate of growing impact). Priority = calculated from Impact + Urgency. Priority determines ticket order and SLA timeframes.

TDX Group vs Individual Assignment Best Practice: Group assignment = ticket visible to all group members, good when multiple technicians can handle it. Individual assignment = single person is primary responsible party. Combined = group visibility + individual accountability. Best practice: always assign to the owning group and keep it there, use individual assignment within that group for accountability. Tickets should not remain unassigned indefinitely.

TDX Escalation Process: If Help Desk cannot resolve, escalate by reassigning to the appropriate Tech group. Add an internal note documenting what was tried, what the result was, and why escalation is needed. The ticket stays in TDX and the new assignee gets notified. Do not close before escalating.

TDX Help Desk First Response Workflow: 1) Create ticket with correct Classification, Type, and Service. 2) Gather info from user (StarID, device name, error message, steps taken). 3) Check AD for account issues. 4) Check SCCM for device status. 5) Search KB for known solutions. 6) Attempt resolution. 7) If unresolved, escalate to Tech group with internal note. 8) Update ticket status throughout.

**TeamDynamix Search & Filter Fields**
TDX allows advanced filtering using the following fields: Requestor (Search by name/StarID), Acct/Dept, Current Status (New, Open, In Process, etc.), Ticket Type, Form, Responsibility (Person or Group), Created Date Range, Start/Due Date, Location/Room, and Source (Phone, Email, etc.). You can also filter by Assets/CIs and 'Only Top-Level' tickets.
